title: Ancient Sheep Helps Solve a Mystery of an Old Plague
summary: A long time ago, a sickness called plague made many people very sick. Scientists have found a clue about how this plague spread thousands of years ago. They discovered the plague bacteria in the bones of a sheep that lived 4,000 years ago in Russia. This sheep was from a place called Arkaim. The scientists think that animals like sheep might have helped the plague move across big areas. This is important because it helps us understand how diseases can travel. The research was done by a team from different countries, and they published their findings in a science journal. By studying old animal bones, they learn about history and health.

background_read: The plague is a disease that has affected people for thousands of years. In the Middle Ages, it caused the Black Death, which killed many in Europe. Scientists study old bones to learn about past diseases. By looking at animal remains, they can find clues about how plagues spread. This helps us protect health today. The discovery of plague in a sheep bone shows that animals might have carried the disease long ago, helping it move across large areas like Eurasia.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists from different countries, including Taylor Hermes from the University of Arkansas
WHAT: Found plague bacteria in a 4,000-year-old sheep bone from Arkaim, Russia
WHERE: Arkaim, a fortified settlement in Russia near Kazakhstan
WHY: To understand how the plague spread during the Bronze Age without fleas, suggesting livestock played a role

perspectives.description: The finding helps explain how ancient plagues spread by showing animals could carry the disease.
